paragraphs.body: Chapter 2

Silverwood College was only a fifteen minute walk from Iris’s apartment. The town of Silverwood was vastly composed of college students and faculty, which Iris appreciated. It made her feel more comfortable and seen, knowing that most of the people around her were just like her, that they wanted to learn and push the boundaries of the known. The weekend passed by in a blur of decorating, putting together furniture with such intricate steps that Iris wondered how anyone did it on their own, and again more takeout from the same spot. It was both relaxing and exhausting, making her own place hers, but it came with that unique satisfaction at the end of each day, seeing it all come together.

annotations.comment: This is a touching depiction of university faculty. :)
Here, you seemed to leap to a different topic within the paragraph — now about the weekend vs. the college.
annotations.context_right: nd passed by in a bl

annotations.text: anyone did it on their own
annotations.comment: But she did, right?

paragraphs.body: Before she knew it classes were starting. Iris wanted to make a good first impression on both her professors and her classmates. She left her house almost an hour early, planning on exploring campus while having ample time to find and snag a good seat in the classroom.

annotations.comment: Maybe you start with the weekend and then move to the college here?

paragraphs.body: Iris was thankful that she managed to pick an apartment close to the main street of town. It was quaint, much more charming than she was used to. The street was teeming with bright colors and life. Shops doors were open, allowing the breeze to flow in. Trees also lined the street, casting some much-needed shade. People could be seen sitting in folding chairs outside cafés, talking and laughing with one another. Iris smiled at people as she passed by, strolling and taking in the warm atmosphere. For the first time in a long time, she found that they actually smiled back.

Iris felt like she had stepped into yet another world when she arrived on campus. She had known about the campus’s history and old-school feel, but she never had seen it all come together in person. The three-story brick colonial buildings were set in a rectangle around a main square, with side paths branching off behind them. A statue of the college’s founder was set in the center of the square, with a small plaque underneath it reading his name, Elias Silverwood. There were already students on campus, even at this hour, entering and exiting the buildings surrounding the square. Iris stood there, drinking it all in. She had finally made it.

annotations.comment: Unclear what this would mean. It’s so interesting that she’s never visited the campus and hasn’t felt the need to since she moved to town.
I think it’s fairly unusual to name a town after a university?
Slightly confusing because there were people out in cafés? So why wouldn’t there be people on campus?

paragraphs.body: Her first class of the day was a mid-level economics class. Iris had always performed well in the subject; both the math and the policy spoke to her, and she felt like she could make real change if she understood the way things worked in the world. Also, she knew that her knowledge and skills would be able to allow her to work elsewhere, in places that were worlds away from Cooperstown.

annotations.comment: Nice little bit of information here.

paragraphs.body: The class was in a medium-sized lecture hall. Tall ceilings stretched up almost two stories. The bright overhead lights almost gave Iris a headache. Seats with small desks were crammed in a semi-circle around a wall of whiteboards and a small standing desk where the professor would be. There were already several students who had claimed seats around the room, so Iris took a seat in the middle, closer to the front, one she thought was the most socially acceptable. She grabbed her laptop out of her backpack and set it up.

annotations.comment: What else can this section do? Does she feel uncomfortable, is she worried about keeping up?

paragraphs.body: The room started to fill up quickly as the start of class approached. One part of Iris hoped that no one would sit next to her and she could just focus on her studies. Another, smaller part was hoping to make some new friends without having to go out of her way. Iris glanced around as rows around her began to be completely full. Why was no one sitting next to her? Was something wrong with her? Iris wrung her hands, noticed her palms were sweaty, and wiped them on her jeans.

annotations.comment: Great, glad we start to see her inner monologue a bit.

paragraphs.body: By the time the professor had walked in and set up, Iris was still surrounded by an empty desk on either side. She could hear talking and laughing through her earbuds as she stared at her phone and willed the time to pass faster.

“Alright guys, it’s officially 9am, so let’s get started…” The professor began, looking at his watch. He was tall, bald with a stern face and glasses, and wore a crisp suit and tie. “I’m Professor Keating, and this is Econ 0400. Now–”

annotations.comment: Wow, fancy for a professor!

paragraphs.body: The creak of the heavy doors caused Iris to inadvertently turn her head. There were a few stragglers still entering the hall. Most of them found seats in the back, except for one girl, who made her way toward the front. Iris turned back around as the footsteps approached, and the small “excuse me”s made their way all the way next to her. Iris looked up as a girl plopped down in the seat next to her, heaving great breaths but trying to hide it. Iris said nothing, she wasn’t even sure what to say, until the girl turned to her, still out of breath.
“Is this Econ 1000?” the girl whispered. She was haphazardly pulling things out of her bag, scrambling to find her laptop or her notebook.
“Um, no, sorry,” Iris replied, still whispering. “This is Econ 0400.”
“Shit, what day is it today? Tuesday?”
“Monday, actually,” Iris replied, whispering as quietly as possible so that she could still hear.
“Oh my god, wait, I think this actually is where I need to be,” the girl said. “This is a Monday and Wednesday class?” She was still huffing in between each breath.
“Yeah, pretty sure. Do you have Professor Keating?” Iris asked.
“Think so,” the girl replied. “Guess I’ll just stay here for today then, and check my schedule later. I like your shirt, by the way.”

annotations.comment: This dialogue is well done. I’m already interested in this girl.

paragraphs.body: “Thanks,” Iris said, wrapping her flannel around her. “I mean, it is syllabus week, right? Professors aren’t supposed to do anything today except look at the syllabus. You should be fine, I think.” Iris glanced up at the professor, who was now projecting the class’s syllabus on the board.

“Sometimes they do,” the girl said. “Depends on the professor.” Their professor certainly looked like the no-frills, stern type. Iris hoped that Professor Keating wouldn’t think badly of her for talking during class.

annotations.comment: Maybe this is covered earlier but I’m wondering how Iris knows this about college already? Is she a transfer student? I like that the girl is correcting her.
We were already told this. Also, yes, the professor hates her for talking.

paragraphs.body: “I’m Iris, by the way,” Iris said.
“Sloane,” the girl, Sloane, replied.

annotations.comment: Don’t need this, we were just told.

paragraphs.body: “Nice to meet you,” Iris said.

After their brief exchange, Iris turned back to pay attention to Professor Keating’s lecture. She had already read the syllabus the moment it had been posted to the class, so nothing he was reciting was new to her. Still, she started taking extra note of everything Professor Keating said, but when she looked around not a single other student was doing the same.

annotations.comment: What does this mean? Is she taking notes?

paragraphs.body: “You know you don’t have to do that, right?” Sloane leaned in and pointed at her freshly opened Google Doc.
“Oh uh, yeah, I know,” Iris said. “I was just making sure.”

“I’ve had this professor before, actually,” Sloane said. “Keating is chill as long as you try. Little terrifying though, I will admit.” 
	“Got it, thanks,” Iris said. She deleted everything off her doc, opting instead to just listen to whatever Professor Keating had to say, at least, until the real lecture started. He had quite the monotone voice, with a slight accent of an origin that Iris couldn’t determine.

annotations.comment: Oh, man, Iris is a big follower!

paragraphs.body: When Iris looked back up, Professor Keating had now started to dive into the course material. Iris happened to glance back over at Sloane and her computer. She was, of course, playing sudoku, eyes glued to her screen. Iris wondered how Sloane could have already had this professor and yet chose to play games instead of paying attention. Sloane caught her eye and gave her a smirk before returning to her game. Iris rolled her eyes and continued typing up the notes.

annotations.comment: Feels aggressive.I like the tension you unpack here, between wanting to be cool with Sloane and (I assume) wanting to learn and get something out of the course. It’s not entirely clear what Iris wants more yet though.

paragraphs.body: By the time class was over, Iris’s eyelids were so heavy she was sure she could sleep for the rest of the day. Professor Keating had droned on and on, trying to make the most of the time they had, as he put it, but Iris was dreading the rest of the semester if that was what the first day had been like. Sloane called out to her as she was putting her laptop away.

annotations.comment: I get it, but how fascinating did she think an Econ class was going to be? (I’m not being sarcastic — did she have hopes it was going to be great?)

paragraphs.body: “Hey,” she said. Iris looked up and saw that Sloane had already slung her bag over her shoulder and was standing above her.
“Hey,” Iris said.
“You’ve got any other classes today?” Sloane asked.
“Uh, yeah, I have one more, but that’s not until three,” Iris said.
“Cool. Wanna maybe, like grab a coffee or something? You seem pretty alright,” Sloane said, staring at her.

annotations.comment: This felt unnecessary.

paragraphs.body: “Oh, uh, yeah sure! I’d like that,” Iris said. “Did you have anywhere in mind?”

“Yeah, there’s this really good coffee place just down the street from campus,” Sloane said. “C’mon, I’ll show you.” She gestured for Iris to follow her as they made their way out of the lecture hall. Iris quickly packed up her things and followed Sloane out.

“So, Iris,” Sloane said. “Let’s just get through the quick college questionnaire. What year are you?” She didn’t even look over at Iris as she walked, which Iris struggled to keep up with in the first place.
“Oh, uh, junior,” Iris said. “But I’m a transfer student, so this is actually my first semester here.” She felt Sloane’s eyes turn to her.

annotations.comment: Ah, okay. I am wondering what Sloane wants out of this conversation.

paragraphs.body: “Oh wow, really? That’s cool!” Sloane said genuinely. “I guess it’s not every day someone transfers here, especially since we’re so small.”

“Yeah,” Iris said, chuckling nervously. “So, what about you?”
	“I’m a senior,” Sloane said. “One more year!” She pumped her fists in the air in mock celebration. “It can’t come soon enough.”
“Yeah, I can definitely relate to that,” Iris said. “Although, haven’t you had a good time here? It seems really nice.”

“I mean yeah, it’s nice here if all you want is to see the same five faces every single day,” said Sloane. “I can’t wait to just get out here and be somewhere different.” Although Iris felt the exact opposite she felt herself nodding along with Sloane.

annotations.comment: If it’s so small, maybe Sloane should be more openly interested in Iris as a new student?

paragraphs.body:
“So, anyway, what’s your major?” said Iris. 
	“Film Studies,” Sloane replied. “Not really sure what I’m gonna do with it, but I want to travel the world, so… first up: traveling the world. Then I’ll be a Hollywood director.” They both laughed. “Anyway, we’re almost here. It’s right there,” Sloane said, pointing up ahead. Iris squinted, making out the words Sally’s Treats . The café was the first of a strip of businesses that ran on a side street of the town’s main road. It was quieter back here, with less traffic Iris could hear only the rustling of tree leaves and the quiet murmuring of those sitting outside and enjoying the midday sun.

annotations.comment: What a touchingly ridiculous plan.
Side note: the college is tiny but the town is named after it?

paragraphs.body: As the two stepped inside the café Iris was hit with the smell of coffee grounds and bread baking. The walls were a striped pastel blue and white, and plants stood on every available shelf, leaves climbing up the walls and hanging down from the shelves. Iris could instantly see why Sloane liked coming here.
“Well, this is it,” Sloane said.
“Wow!” Iris said. “It is so cute in here!”
“The best part is that the drinks and pastries aren’t terribly overpriced like they are everywhere else,” Sloane said.

“Great,” Iris replied enthusiastically. 
	“I’ll give you a second to look over things, I’ll order and get us a table,” Sloane said, leaving Iris and approaching the counter. Iris contemplated for a minute before just deciding on a random menu item. She had never been to coffee shops back home, as she was always too busy or not allowed out. Now she was going to change that.

annotations.comment: So, this is well written. But I’m also not quite sure where it’s going? Iris has made a friend, which is lovely — but what’s the plot?
annotations.context_left:
“Great,” Iris replied enthusiastically. 
	“I’ll give you a second to look over things, I’ll order
annotations.context_right: get us a table,” Sloane said, leaving Iris and approaching the counter. Iris contemplated for a min

annotations.comment: Oh, it sounded to me like Sloane was ordering for both of them.

paragraphs.body: Iris ordered her drink as smoothly as possible (spoiler: she pronounced americano wrong) and went to sit with Sloane at a table by the window. “What’d you get?” she asked.
“An iced latte. Nothing crazy,” Sloane said. “So, anyway, I wanna hear more about you. What’s your major? Why did you decide to transfer?”
“Uh, yeah, so I’m a finance major and I’m gonna try to minor in history,” Iris said, looking down.
“Got it, cool,” Sloane replied. “So, what made you decide to come here?” Sloane gestured at the room.

annotations.comment: Why is Sloane so interested in Iris?

paragraphs.body: “It’s a pretty long story,” Iris said, shaking it off. “I could only afford community college before this, and now that I transferred to a real university I wanted something… I don’t know, quieter? Somewhere small, out of the way of things.” She hoped that was enough information for Sloane to stop going down that rabbit hole.
“Oh, yeah, totally get it,” Sloane said. “I have a friend from home who did something similar.” Iris hoped for the friend’s sake that their situations were not similar.

annotations.comment: It might be interesting if she said this aloud, or something like it.

paragraphs.body: “So, why are you in our class anyway? If you’re a film major, I mean.” Iris wondered how someone so ready to get out of school would want to be stuck in a mid-level economics class.

“My parents wanted me to take on an econ minor,” Sloane said, rolling her eyes. “Something about wanting me to understand the real world if I’m gonna insist on a film major. Blah blah blah, the only reason I did what they asked is because they’re basically paying for me to be here. I guess I could take some boring classes here and there. It’s too bad I saved the majority of them for this year.” She sighed. “It’s gonna be rough, but at least I now know someone in one of my classes, huh?” They both chuckled briefly. “What about you?”

annotations.comment: Feels off since she’s been complaining about having the same 5 people in her classes?

paragraphs.body: “I guess I just like economics,” Iris said, shrugging her shoulders. “Technically I’m a finance major, but this course is counting towards that.”
“I could never do that,” Sloane replied. “Good for you, though.” Just then, the waiter soon brought over Sloane’s latte and Iris’s americano. She took a sip and nearly gagged.
“Oh my god,” Iris said. “Is this even coffee?”

“Well, yeah,” Sloane said, laughing. “You got an americano, right? Do you like coffee?”
	“Clearly not as much as I thought,” Iris said, motioning to the barista for a glass of water. “What’s in an americano anyway?”
“When was the last time you went to a coffee shop?” Sloane asked.

annotations.comment: Nice.

paragraphs.body: “I…” Iris paused. “Not sure. I-I think it’s been a while. I’m not really a coffee person but I thought I’d try something new.” She forced out a chuckle.
“Well, you definitely picked one of the worst drinks to try to start drinking,” Sloane said, laughing. “Why don’t you order a latte? It’s a lot sweeter than an americano.”

Iris agreed, and once her drink arrived they sat there for a while, talking about their classes, how boring Professor Keating was, and whether they thought this year was going to be hard.The latte was better, but not by much. She reluctantly sipped away at it, trying to not let it go to waste. After their drinks were empty and the conversation waned, Iris made a move to leave.

annotations.comment: Again, I get the sense that you don’t know what to do with these two. You have a nice little set up — so what can happen now?

paragraphs.body: “Well,” she said. “I should probably go home and grab some food before I head to my next class.”
“Yeah, I’ll get going too,” Sloane said, standing up with her empty drink cup in hand. “I had a nice time talking to you.”
“Me too,” Iris replied, brushing her hair to the side.
“Hey, would you be open to hanging out tomorrow? I could show you around campus, go in all the buildings, you know, show you the ropes.” She winked.
“That would be great,” Iris replied. “Thank you so much.”

annotations.comment: Why don’t these just do this now?

paragraphs.body: “No problem,” Sloane said. “I love taking people under my wing. I’ll see you around then.” With that, Sloane shot Iris some finger guns, turned around and waltzed out of the café, her partially unzipped bag slung over her shoulder. Iris almost called out, but then Sloane was gone faster than she could say anything. She returned her cup to the counter, took a deep breath and left the café into the afternoon sunshine.

The rest of the day passed in a blur of rushing from the house back to campus only to get lost and be late to her second class. Iris was definitely going to need Sloane’s help to find her way around, all the buildings were ancient and their number systems were annoying and nonsensical. She almost got lost after her second class of the day, and by the time she found her way out there was a noticeable change in the weather. Instead of the sweltering heat typical of August, it had cooled to a bearable temperature. Iris found a bench where she could grab her keys out of her bag before she headed home. When she unzipped her bag, a neon yellow sticky note lay on top of her sweater. It read:
	MEET ME IN LINCOLN. 12PM. ROOM 106.

annotations.text: the day passed in a blur of
annotations.comment: Summary is nice.
I was confused by this since it’s a small school and she had no trouble finding her first classroom?

paragraphs.body: Underneath the meeting place, Sloane had scribbled down her number. It was slightly smudged, but Iris could still make it out. She smiled to herself. She folded the sticky note neatly and tucked it into her pocket. She was already thinking about seeing Sloane tomorrow.

annotations.comment: Is there a romantic vibe between them? I didn’t get that until here. Eleanor, I enjoyed this excerpt — you seem more confident and your pacing is a lot better here. I did wonder why exactly we needed to see the scene in the coffee shop in such detail? And also how all of this connects tot he section we read previously? The guy in that part seems to have disappeared! Interesting work — think about plot. What are the events that make up the story? What’s the conflict?
